analysis:
• Markets price near-zero chance of ceasefire by end of March (2-3% across all contracts), with military action through March 31 at 95%. The April 30 ceasefire contract at 39% and April 15 at 20-22% suggest traders see a narrow but real window for a deal in the next 2-4 weeks, likely tied to the April 6 energy-strike pause deadline Trump set on March 26.

• Key catalyst this week: Iran rejected the US 15-point ceasefire proposal as "maximalist, unreasonable" and issued its own counterproposal demanding reparations, sovereignty guarantees, and a halt to targeted killings. No direct talks have occurred — all communication is through mediators (Pakistan, others). US envoy Witkoff claims meetings could happen "this week," but Iran's foreign minister explicitly denied any plans for negotiations.

• Trump extended the pause on Iranian energy infrastructure strikes by 10 days (to April 6), framing it as a goodwill gesture amid "productive" back-channel progress. This is the second extension, and it's keeping oil markets from worst-case pricing — but the pause's expiry creates a binary catalyst that could sharply move the April 7 and April 15 ceasefire contracts.

• The ceasefire-by-December contract at 76% and conflict-ends-by-December at 83% imply traders are highly confident this ends within 2026, but the mid-range pricing (May 31 at 55%, June 30 at 63-64%) suggests the base case is a protracted 2-3 month wind-down rather than a quick resolution. The $35.8M volume on the March 31 ceasefire contract dwarfs later dates, reflecting heavy speculative interest in near-term resolution that has now largely resolved to "no."

• Regime-change risk is non-trivial: Polymarket prices Iranian regime collapse before 2027 at 34%, reflecting the elimination of Khamenei on Feb 28 and ongoing degradation of Iranian military infrastructure (10,000+ US airstrikes, 120+ historical sites damaged). This complicates ceasefire dynamics — it's unclear who has authority to negotiate on Iran's behalf.

• On-the-ground escalation continues to pressure timelines: 10 US service members were injured in an Iranian attack on Prince Sultan Air Base in Saudi Arabia on March 27, and Israeli operations in Lebanon have killed over 1,100 people since March 2. The conflict's geographic spread beyond Iran proper makes a clean ceasefire mechanically harder, supporting the market's skepticism about near-term resolution.

Sources:
